regions.name: Gothic Quarter & El Born
regions.description: Barcelona's medieval heart — labyrinthine Roman streets, Gothic cathedral, and El Born's trendy bars and boutiques
regions.bestFor: Medieval history, nightlife, tapas, boutique shopping

regions.name: Eixample — Gaudí District
regions.description: 19th-century grid district home to Gaudí's masterpieces — Sagrada Família, Casa Batlló, and Casa Milà
regions.bestFor: Modernist architecture, luxury shopping, upscale restaurants

regions.name: Barceloneta & Montjuïc
regions.description: Beach, Olympic stadium, castle, and Montjuïc gardens — Barcelona's outdoor playground
regions.bestFor: Beach days, panoramic views, outdoor activities

attractions.name: Sagrada Família
attractions.description: Gaudí's breathtaking unfinished basilica — a UNESCO World Heritage Site under construction since 1882, towering above the city

attractions.name: Park Güell
attractions.description: Gaudí's fairy-tale hilltop park with mosaic terraces, curved colonnades, and panoramic city views

attractions.name: Casa Batlló
attractions.description: Gaudí's dragon-spine masterpiece on Passeig de Gràcia — one of the world's most imaginative building facades

attractions.name: Gothic Quarter (Barri Gòtic)
attractions.description: 2,000-year-old Roman ruins embedded in medieval streets — Barcelona Cathedral, Temple d'August, and Plaça Reial

attractions.name: La Boqueria Market
attractions.description: Barcelona's famous covered market on La Rambla — vibrant stalls of fresh produce, jamón, seafood, and smoothies

attractions.name: Picasso Museum
attractions.description: Largest collection of Picasso's early works set across five medieval palaces — traces his formative years in Barcelona

attractions.name: Barceloneta Beach
attractions.description: Barcelona's urban beach — 4.5km of golden sand, beach bars (chiringuitos), and Mediterranean vibes

attractions.name: Montjuïc Castle & Gardens
attractions.description: Historic hilltop fortress with sweeping harbour views — reachable by cable car for extra drama

attractions.name: Casa Milà (La Pedrera)
attractions.description: Gaudí's undulating apartment building on Passeig de Gràcia — the rooftop terrace with warrior chimney stacks is iconic

attractions.name: El Born & Palau de la Música
attractions.description: Trendy neighbourhood with tapas bars, and the breathtaking Art Nouveau concert hall built in 1908

pros: Almost no tourist queues at Sagrada Família or Park Güell

notableEvents: Sant Jordi Day (April 23rd — Barcelona's Valentine's Day)
pros: Sant Jordi Day is magical — city fills with books and roses

cons: Very hot midday — siesta time is real

culturalTips: Dinner in Barcelona starts at 9-10pm — arriving at 7pm marks you as a tourist; restaurants often aren't fully open until 8:30pm
The Catalan people are proud of their distinct identity — saying 'Gràcies' (Catalan) instead of 'Gracias' is appreciated
Book Sagrada Família and Casa Batlló tickets weeks or months in advance — they regularly sell out
Tapas are meant for sharing — order several small plates and share across the table
Las Ramblas is tourist-heavy and a pickpocket hotspot — keep valuables secure and explore the side streets
Sunday mornings are quiet and atmospheric — perfect for Gothic Quarter walks before crowds arrive
The beach is walkable from the Gothic Quarter — it is free and easily accessible
Patxaran and Cava (Catalan sparkling wine) are the local drinks — order them confidently

mid.description: Charming, central, boutique hotels, close to Gothic Quarter and Eixample, $130-220/night

luxury.area: Passeig de Gràcia or Barceloneta waterfront
luxury.description: Design hotels, rooftop pools, sea or city views, $280-600/night

El Born: El Born & Palau de la Música
